name: Bleeding Hill Mine
short_description: Historic mine site in Crawford County offering opportunities to find quartz, calcite, and fluorite specimens. Flat terrain makes this accessible for rockhounders of varying skill levels.
description:
Bleeding Hill Mine is a historic mineral locality in Crawford County, Missouri, situated in the heart of the Ozark region's rich mining heritage. The site is named for its distinctive reddish iron oxide staining on exposed surfaces, which gives the area its characteristic appearance. Collectors here can search for quartz crystals, calcite specimens, and fluorite in various colors - white, purple, and occasionally green varieties. The flat terrain and relatively accessible location make this an appealing destination for families and beginners interested in Missouri's mining history.

The Ozark region of Missouri has been mined for minerals and metals for over a century. The geology here reflects ancient hydrothermal activity that filled fractures and cavities in dolomite and limestone formations with crystalline minerals. Fluorite in particular was extensively mined throughout Crawford County and the surrounding area, making historical mine sites like Bleeding Hill valuable for understanding the region's geological and industrial past.

Visitors should be prepared for uneven ground around old mine workings and respect the historical nature of the site. The relatively flat elevation of 838 feet keeps the terrain manageable year-round, though spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. Always practice safe rockhounding: watch for loose rock near old excavations, avoid entering sealed or unstable mine shafts, and collect responsibly.
history: This area sits within the Ozark mining district of Missouri, where Paleozoic dolomite and limestone host hydrothermal mineral deposits. Fluorite, galena, and sphalerite were extensively mined here during the 19th and 20th centuries, and Bleeding Hill represents one of many historic mine sites that define Missouri's mineral-rich character.
tips: Search the tailings piles and exposed cut banks for quartz geodes and calcite crystals - the reddish iron oxide staining makes it easier to spot mineral-bearing rock. Use a 10x loupe to examine specimens for the purple fluorite and clear quartz that this site is known for. The best finds typically come from carefully examining loose rubble rather than attempting to extract fresh material from unstable formations.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at 838 feet elevation with sparse vegetation typical of the Ozark plateau. Walking surfaces are uneven around old mine workings with scattered rubble and exposed soil. Parking area is likely unpaved. Expect a short walk from parking to the main collecting area.
best_season: March - May, September - November. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures and lower humidity. Summer heat and occasional heavy rains can make the site muddy and less enjoyable. Winter conditions are generally mild but can be wet.
written_directions: From Steelville, take Missouri State Highway 8 east for approximately 4 miles toward the Crawford County mining district. Watch for local roads and signs indicating historic mine sites. GPS coordinates (38.041413, -91.153894) will bring you to the general area. The site may be on private or historic preserve land - confirm access and permissions locally before visiting.
road_conditions: Unpaved road access likely; verify conditions before visiting
parking_notes: Limited informal parking near mine site; may be unstaffed and unmarked
collection_rules: This is a historic site on private or preserve land. Always obtain permission from the current land owner before collecting. No motorized excavation. Respect mine safety - do not enter closed shafts or unstable areas. Collect only loose material from the surface.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission before collecting
No motorized equipment
Do not enter sealed or unstable mine shafts
Collect surface specimens only
Respect the historic nature of the site
Remove all trash
nearest_services: Steelville is approximately 4 miles west with gas, food, and water available
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven ground around old mine workings requires sturdy footwear. Avoid unstable excavations. Short walk from parking area. Limited wheelchair accessibility due to uneven rubble.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Bleeding Hill Mine in Missouri?
faq.answer: Bleeding Hill Mine is known for quartz crystals, calcite specimens, fluorite (in white, purple, and occasionally green), and galena. The site's distinctive reddish iron oxide staining makes it easier to spot mineral-bearing rock on the surface.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Bleeding Hill Mine free or do you need a fee?
faq.answer: Bleeding Hill Mine is located on private or historic preserve land, so there is no standard fee. However, you must obtain permission from the current landowner before collecting. Contact local rockhounding clubs in Crawford County for access information.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Bleeding Hill Mine?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. Spring and fall temperatures and lower humidity make for pleasant rockhounding, while summer can be hot and wet, and winter conditions may be muddy.

faq.question: Is Bleeding Hill Mine beginner-friendly for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, the flat terrain at 838 feet elevation and relatively accessible location make this site suitable for beginners and families. Surface collecting is straightforward, though you should avoid old mine workings and unstable areas.

faq.question: What tools do you need for rockhounding at Bleeding Hill Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a 10x loupe to examine quartz and fluorite specimens, a small hammer and chisel for surface collection, a sturdy backpack, and good boots for the uneven rubble-covered terrain. A bucket or collection bag helps carry your finds.

faq.question: Are there camping and amenities near Bleeding Hill Mine in Crawford County?
faq.answer: Steelville, approximately 4 miles west, offers gas, food, and water. The Ozark National Scenic Waterways and nearby state parks provide camping options. Plan ahead for lodging and supplies before heading to the remote mine site.

meta_description: Find quartz, fluorite, and calcite at Bleeding Hill Mine in Crawford County, Missouri. Historic mine site on private land. Beginner-friendly rockhounding.
